Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd usually just study when I felt like it or when something was due soon, maybe putting in five hours a week if that. I never really had a set schedule—I'd just squeeze in reading or notes whenever I had a gap between my extracurriculars or when the anxiety about failing got too intense. Most of the time I was just winging it.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly cram when an assignment was due. Regular study times just never clicked for me—there was always something else going on, or I just couldn't bring myself to sit down and focus until the pressure was really on.
Q3: Usually what got me to log in was either a deadline notification or just checking if there was something urgent I'd missed. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what was happening, scroll around a bit without really engaging. The anxiety of not knowing what was due would push me to check, but once I saw the workload, I'd sometimes just close it again.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me. I'd have bursts where I'd log in more regularly, especially early in the semester when I told myself I'd stay on top of things. But that motivation faded fast, and it just became something I used reactively rather than proactively.
Q5: The reading materials were probably most useful since I learn better by reading and writing things out. If there were clear notes or PDFs, I could download them and work through them at my own pace. The videos were okay but honestly I'd zone out watching them—reading just stuck better for me.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums. Posting there felt pointless and I never really saw the value in it—I only made like two posts the whole course. I also skipped a lot of the optional readings and supplementary materials because I was already struggling to keep up with the required stuff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help me pass, I'd do it. Anything that seemed like busywork or extra fluff, I just couldn't justify spending energy on when I was already stretched thin with everything else going on.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely skippable to me. Required activities had consequences if I didn't do them, so there was actual pressure. Optional activities just fell to the bottom of my list and stayed there. With my anxiety already through the roof about the required work, adding optional stuff felt impossible.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download PDFs and readings, then study from those on paper or on my laptop without being connected. Since I don't have reliable internet access at home, a lot of my actual studying happened offline with printed notes and materials I'd saved when I was on campus.
Q10: A lot of my studying happened with physical notes I'd written out from downloaded materials. I'd sit with those for a while, rewriting key concepts, trying to memorize stuff. None of that shows up anywhere in the system. Same with reviewing my own notes before exams—that's invisible work.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture PDF or reading once, then never open it again in the LMS because I had it saved locally. My actual engagement with that material happened entirely offline, sometimes over multiple days, but the system would just show one quick access.
Q12: There were definitely times when everything felt overwhelming. When multiple assignments piled up or when my anxiety was really bad, I'd just shut down and avoid the course entirely. The stress would build up and I'd feel paralyzed, unable to even open the LMS without feeling sick about how behind I was.
Q13: Absolutely. Fatigue and competing responsibilities hit me hard. Between extracurriculars and just general life stuff with limited resources, I often came to the LMS already exhausted. Confusion about what was expected also made me avoid things—if I didn't understand an assignment, I'd put it off rather than ask for help.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd open videos or readings and just stare at them without anything sinking in. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things or just too tired to process. I'd technically be 'engaging' with the material but getting absolutely nothing from it.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even important ones. The anxiety made it worse—the bigger the assignment, the more I'd avoid it because starting felt overwhelming. I knew I was hurting myself but I just couldn't break the cycle. It's like my brain would freeze up when facing something difficult.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually do work. I'd ignore the LMS for days, then frantically log in and try to complete everything in the final hours before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the only reliable motivator I had.
Q17: Feedback sometimes helped, sometimes made things worse. If I got decent feedback, I'd feel a brief boost of motivation. But if it was critical or I did poorly, the anxiety would spike and I'd avoid the course even more. It was hard to use feedback constructively when I was already struggling emotionally.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with 17 courses to manage. Having so many things to track made it easy to lose stuff in the chaos. I needed more rigid structure with clearer priorities, but instead everything felt equally demanding and I couldn't figure out what to focus on first.
Q19: The lack of clear guidance on what was most important made engagement harder. When everything seems equally weighted, I'd just pick whatever was due soonest and ignore the rest. Clearer pathways or priority markers would have helped me figure out where to put my limited energy.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in repeatedly out of anxiety, clicking around, maybe rewatching videos without actually working on submissions. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just spinning your wheels, too stressed to actually produce anything.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I still passed with decent exam scores because I was studying offline with downloaded materials. The system doesn't capture the work I did with printed notes at home or in the library without internet.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on. But they miss everything that happens offline, and they definitely can't measure the quality of engagement. Someone could watch a video twice and learn nothing, or read notes once and understand everything. The numbers don't capture that.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're mentally present and the learning sticks. For me, real engagement was rare this semester—mostly I was just trying to survive and pass rather than genuinely connecting with the content.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety affected my engagement, or how much I actually learned from offline studying. It wouldn't capture the CBT sessions I did to try to manage my stress, or how my limited resources and lack of home internet shaped how I accessed materials. There's a whole person behind those login numbers.
